title: Streams & Buffers
summary: Process data piece by piece instead of all at once — the key to handling big files and network data efficiently.

blocks.md: What if you need to read a 5 GB file? Loading it fully into memory would blow up. **Streams** let you process data in small **chunks** as it arrives — constant memory, no matter the size. They're everywhere in Node: files, HTTP requests/responses, compression, and more.

A **Buffer** is a fixed-length chunk of raw binary data (bytes). Streams hand you Buffers; you turn them into text with `.toString()` or an encoding. Binary data (images, video, network packets) lives in Buffers.

blocks.caption: reading a file as a stream

blocks.md: Instead of one giant read, you get many `'data'` events, each a manageable chunk. `'end'` fires when the source is exhausted; `'error'` if something fails. This is a **Readable** stream — data flows out of it.

blocks.caption: pipe a readable into a writable

blocks.title: pipe() handles backpressure
blocks.md: `.pipe()` connects a Readable to a Writable and automatically manages **backpressure** — if the destination can't keep up, it pauses the source. It's the idiomatic, safe way to move stream data (a web server streaming a file to a client is exactly this).

blocks.question: Why use a stream to read a very large file instead of fs.readFile?
options: Streams are always faster for tiny files
Streams process the file in small chunks, keeping memory usage low regardless of file size
readFile doesn't work on large files
blocks.explanation: fs.readFile loads the entire file into memory at once. A stream delivers small chunks over time, so memory stays low even for huge files — essential for large files and network data.

keyTakeaways: Streams process data in chunks over time, keeping memory usage flat for any size.
A Buffer is a fixed block of raw bytes; use .toString(encoding) to read it as text.
`.pipe()` connects a readable to a writable and handles backpressure automatically.
Files, HTTP, and compression are all built on streams.

exercise.prompt: Using streams, how would you copy 'a.txt' to 'b.txt' without ever holding the whole file in memory? Write the one-liner.
